React Was Initially Hated and Ridiculed by the Web Development Community
React JS, now a cornerstone of modern web development, faced immense backlash when it was first introduced at JS Conf US in 2013. The prevailing sentiment at the time was strong adherence to the “Separation of Concerns” principle, where HTML, CSS, and JavaScript were strictly separated in different files. React’s introduction of JSX, allowing developers to write XML-like syntax directly within JavaScript, was seen as a major violation of this rule.
Why Was it Hated?
The criticism centered on the blending of markup and logic. Detractors labeled JSX as a “monstrosity” and a “step backward.” They believed it would lead to unmaintainable spaghetti code and erase the hard-earned benefits of separating structure, presentation, and behavior. The idea of re-rendering the entire UI on every state change also seemed inefficient and preposterous to many.
The Road to Acceptance
Despite the initial resistance, the React team persevered. They engaged with critics, addressed concerns, and demonstrated the practical benefits of their approach. Over time, developers began to appreciate the component-based architecture, the efficiency of the virtual DOM, and the power of mixing markup with JavaScript for data-driven UIs. The strong adoption by major companies like Instagram and later the rest of Facebook further validated React’s potential.
